overall_feedback: Solid pre-submission draft. Compiles cleanly, no undefined refs, careful attribution to Schlag-Tremewan, honest 'no universally best rule' framing. Main weaknesses: hardcoded section/subsection numbers (Section~5 actually points to subsection 5.2); mixed UK/US spelling; the Manhattan threshold-search claim of unimodality is unproved; abstract slightly compresses the analytical crossover (which is in m, not directly in alpha).

comments.title: Hardcoded section/subsection numbers — Section~5 actually points to subsection 5.2
comments.quote: two further count-loss rules---Hamming and Chebyshev distance---violate it and Section~5 takes them up as the boundary of the approach.
comments.explanation: Six places say 'Section~5' for Hamming/Chebyshev, but in the compiled PDF those rules live in subsection 5.2 of Discussion. Reference resolves but is brittle and slightly misleading; identical issue for Section~2/3/4 hardcoding throughout.
comments.fix: Add \label{sec:hamming-chebyshev} to subsection 5.2 (and labels to all other sections); replace literal 'Section~5' with \ref{}. Use \Cref/\ref everywhere section numbers appear.

comments.title: Unimodality of the Manhattan threshold optimum is asserted but not proved
comments.quote: Each sharp coordinate bound is then the optimum, over c in [0,1], of a function that is unimodal in c, so it is the solution of a single monotone scalar equation.
comments.explanation: Section 3.4 leans on unimodality to justify the one-dimensional root-find. The appendix proof of Proposition 4 derives the threshold representation but stops short of proving the optimization is unimodal. A referee will ask for this.
comments.fix: Add a short lemma or remark in app:manhattan-proof proving unimodality of the per-coordinate sup/inf function in c (or cite the binomial-CDF monotonicity argument that delivers it). Reproducible script can also be referenced for the monotone root-find.

comments.title: Abstract overstates analytic crossover by collapsing m and alpha
comments.quote: squared-distance scoring when beliefs are concentrated on a few categories, frequency-guessing scoring when they are balanced across them, with a crossover between the regimes that is established analytically for the closed-form rules and quantified by a design comparison.
comments.explanation: Corollary 1 establishes a crossover analytically in m = number of touched categories. The link 'm tracks belief concentration alpha' is empirical (Section 4), not analytical. The abstract reads as if the crossover in alpha itself were analytical.
comments.fix: Rephrase abstract to either (a) state the analytic result is in report concentration m, with the simulation linking m to alpha; or (b) say 'with a crossover... established analytically in report concentration and quantified, in belief concentration, by a design comparison.' The intro line 50 already does this carefully — bring the abstract in line.

comments.title: Inconsistent UK/US spelling — systematic across files
comments.quote: minimises worst-case regret ... behaviour ... favourable ... centre-biased ... centerpiece ... minimizes ... behavior ... optimizing
comments.explanation: Mix: 'favourable', 'minimises', 'behaviour', 'centre-biased' (UK) coexist with 'centerpiece' (intro), 'minimizes' (multiple), 'behavior' (appendix 299), 'optimizing'. Five-plus instances of the same class of issue, so escalated to one Major rather than several Minors.
comments.fix: Pick one variant — paper is mostly UK (European Economic Review / Economic Journal cites, 'behaviour', 'favours'). Convert 'centerpiece' → 'centrepiece', 'minimize(s)' → 'minimise(s)', 'optimize(s)' → 'optimise(s)', 'behavior' → 'behaviour', 'characterize(s)' → 'characterise(s)'.

comments.title: Abstract phrase 'per-coordinate expected loss' is imprecise for frequency-guessing
comments.quote: All three are instances of a single structural condition on the per-coordinate expected loss
comments.explanation: For frequency-guessing the objective is the multinomial probability (a product), not a per-coordinate expected loss. The body (03_scoring_rules.tex:17–18) is explicit that the loss is not additively separable but becomes so after a log transformation. The abstract collapses this.
comments.fix: Rephrase as 'All three reduce to a separable resource-allocation problem with discrete-convex per-coordinate cost (after a log transformation for frequency-guessing); two further count-loss rules ... violate the conditions.' This stays one sentence and matches the body's framing.

comments.title: \DeltaK macro not used in introduction
comments.explanation: The macro \DeltaK is defined and used in every other section. The introduction (line 26) writes \Delta^k literally. Cosmetic but easy fix.

comments.title: Informal hedges 'something clean', 'this binary case is clean'
comments.quote: Parts (1) and (2) together say something clean ... This binary case is clean.
comments.explanation: 'Clean' is conversational. Acceptable in an exposition paragraph but stands out against the otherwise precise tone.
comments.fix: Rephrase 3:225 to 'Parts (1) and (2) jointly imply that the comparison between the closed-form rules is determined entirely by m.' And 3:271 to 'The binary case admits a closed-form description.'

comments.title: Two minor overfull hboxes (~2pt and ~1.8pt)
comments.quote: Overfull \hbox (2.19576pt too wide) in paragraph at lines 239--241 ... Overfull \hbox (1.76166pt too wide) in paragraph at lines 332--63
comments.explanation: Both well under 10pt — purely cosmetic. First is the binomial CDF notation paragraph in 03_scoring_rules.tex around line 239; second is the Robustness paragraph in 08_appendix.tex.
comments.fix: Either ignore (paper compiles cleanly) or insert a hyphenation hint / soft \linebreak. Lowest priority.

comments.title: Abstract says 'three' rules in §3 but it is in fact §3.1–3.4 with a corollary in 3.3
comments.quote: The next section applies this setup to the three headline rules---squared-distance, frequency-guessing, and Manhattan---beginning with a structural lemma that links them.
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 is the comparison corollary, sitting between Frequency-Guessing (3.2) and Manhattan (3.4). A reader hitting 3.3 expects the third rule but gets a comparison — minor flow surprise.
comments.fix: Either move subsection 3.3 to after 3.4, or add a one-sentence forward pointer in 3.2 ('Before turning to Manhattan in §3.4, we first compare the two closed-form rules.'). Trivial fix.

comments.title: SchlagTremewanSimple bibkey is opaque
comments.explanation: Bib key 'SchlagTremewanSimple' is a stub-style key; the published article is in J. Risk and Uncertainty 2021. Standard practice is YEAR-suffixed keys like 'SchlagTremewan2021'.
comments.fix: Rename to SchlagTremewan2021 in references.bib and update all \citet/\citep occurrences (intro 43,59; §3.2 line 127; etc.). Optional but recommended for consistency with other keys.

comments.title: Table notes lack significance qualifiers / units / sample size statement
comments.quote: Win share by belief concentration alpha: the share of belief draws on which a rule gives the narrowest interval, averaged over all n and k at each alpha.
comments.explanation: Both Table 1 (tab:regime-wins) and Table 2 (tab:regime-regret) describe the metric but do not state the per-cell draw count (5,000) or the total N underlying each row. A reader looking only at the table needs to flip back to text.
comments.fix: Append 'Each alpha row aggregates 5,000 draws per (n,k) cell across n in {5,10,20,50} and k in {2,3,5,10}, i.e., 80,000 draws per row.' to both captions.

comments.title: Mechanism claim in §4 stated more confidently than the evidence supports
comments.quote: The crossover has a mechanism in the projection interpretation of squared-distance scoring. A subject reports the integer count vector closest to np; when beliefs are sparse, np lies near a vertex of the simplex, the feasible reports around it are extreme count vectors, and the projection polytope is tight, so the induced bounds are narrow.
comments.explanation: This is presented as an explanation of WHY squared-distance wins for sparse beliefs. It is geometrically plausible but no formal argument is given — it sits between an intuition and a derivation. Reads as a proof-by-picture.
comments.fix: Hedge: 'A geometric intuition for this pattern is...' rather than 'The crossover has a mechanism in...'. Alternatively, derive the projection-polytope tightness claim as a lemma (relating m and alpha analytically).
